subject: Urgent: Verify your account immediately
body:
Dear Employee,

We have detected unusual activity on your account. Your access will be suspended in 24 hours unless you verify your credentials immediately.

Click here to verify: http://company-login.verify-now.tk/login

If you do not verify, your account will be permanently deleted.

IT Support Team
Company Helpdesk

red_flags.text: Suspicious sender domain (company-helpdesk.net, not your company domain)
Threat of account deletion to create panic

explanation: Classic phishing email using urgency, fear, and a spoofed sender. Real IT departments never ask for credentials via email links.

subject: Your package could not be delivered - action required
body:
Dear customer,

We attempted to deliver your package (tracking: NL987654321) but were unable to complete delivery.

To reschedule delivery, a small fee of €2.95 is required.

Pay now: https://postnl-delivery.payment-secure.online/pay?id=NL987654321

Payment must be completed within 48 hours or the package will be returned.

PostNL Customer Service

red_flags.text: Payment URL not official PostNL domain (payment-secure.online)
Small fee request — classic smishing/phishing tactic

explanation: Delivery phishing is extremely common in the Netherlands. Legitimate PostNL emails come from @postnl.nl and never request payment via external links.

subject: Re: Q4 Budget Review - Action needed
body:
Hi,

As discussed in yesterday's meeting, I need you to process an urgent wire transfer of €45,000 to our new supplier before end of day.

Bank: IBAN NL91ABNA0417164300
Reference: Q4-SUPPLIER-2026

This is time-sensitive and needs to happen before the board meeting tomorrow. Please confirm once done. Keep this between us for now as the deal is not yet public.

Thanks,
Jan

Sent from iPhone

red_flags.text: Extreme urgency: 'before end of day', 'before board meeting'
Wire transfer request via email — bypasses normal approval
'As discussed' references fake meeting to build false familiarity

explanation: Business Email Compromise (BEC) — most costly SE attack. Impersonates executives, uses urgency and secrecy to bypass normal financial controls. Always verify wire transfers via phone to a known number.
